focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Rain had a way of making Seoul look laminated. The bus stop glass, the crosswalk paint, the bright fake smiles on campaign posters, everything shone too hard and then went dull when you touched it. Ina stood under the striped awning outside Haneul Self Storage with her mother's keyring cutting half moons into her palm. The keys were older than the building. Eun-sook had wrapped one in red thread, and the thread had gone dark with years of skin oil, like dried blood.

Aunt Hyejin came up the sidewalk in rubber kitchen clogs and a church cardigan carrying fryer grease and mothballs. The odor reached Ina before Hyejin did and dragged up a church-basement memory: flood buckets, wet hymnals, Hyejin assigning work before anyone finished saying hello. Her reading glasses hung on their chain and bounced against her sternum while she walked. She always moved as if she had one more tray to carry and no one else would carry it right.

"You're still here," Hyejin said. She looked at the keyring and then at Ina's face. "Good. I almost decided for both of us and went back home."

Ina heard the part Hyejin did not say: you almost ran. She had, three times, once at every crosswalk on the way here.

"You tried to," Ina said.

"I did." Hyejin tucked wet hair behind her ear. "Then I remembered where she hid the title to my old Matiz. Rice cooker. Inner basket. Your mother did not trust drawers."

She said it like a joke, but her mouth stayed flat.

Ina pushed the glass door. A little bell rang, and the front desk clerk glanced up from a handheld fan pointed at his own face. He wore a Tottenham cap with the brim stained white from salt, and he had three tiny bandages on the same finger, wrapped one above another, as if he kept cutting himself in exactly the same place. prev_tail_excerpt:
...Ji-eun fell asleep in uneven drops, jerking awake twice at hallway noises, then finally staying down with one hand under her cheek and the other curled around nothing.

Ina sat at the floor table with the contract rider, a notebook, and her phone on airplane mode except for one encrypted channel. She listed names: labor lawyer in Nampo who owed Hyejin a favor from a church bookkeeping mess, journalist in Osaka who loved contract abuse stories, former idol who had won a partial debt challenge and now sold vocal warm-up videos online to pay legal bills.

She wrote numbers in the margin. 320,000,000 principal. Estimated filing costs. Emergency housing. Family transfer buffer if Ji-eun's mother got targeted. Every figure looked obscene and necessary.

Around midnight, the rain stopped. Market trucks started up again below, engines coughing to life one by one. Ina flexed her left wrist where the burn scar tightened in damp weather and kept writing until the page edges grew soft under her hand.

She had missed the council summons. She had ignored direct orders. She had put a public figure under unauthorized protection in a city full of cameras. She had promised money she did not have and legal victories she had not earned.

For the first time since Jamsil, the panic in her chest shifted shape. It was not gone; it had been reassigned.

Across the room, Ji-eun slept without pills, mouth slightly open, hair drying in rough waves against the borrowed pillow. Every few minutes, her left hand twitched once, then stilled.

Ina watched that hand until dawn diluted the window from black to gray. When the gulls started up again and the first fish auction shout bounced off the alley walls, she opened a new page in the notebook and wrote at the top:

GET HER OUT, THEN PAY WHAT IT COSTS.
